speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Charles Jelleberg was a teamster and Carl Lancaster an all -around logger for potlatch forests.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Both were raised in the county and they are long time friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they get together they swap stories about what they did and saw and found memorable working in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Much of what they say here has to do with horses and their treatment and with various jobs, accidents and relations between loggers. The conversation begins with Charles recalling the family homesteading at Park.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I'd like to have you tell me what you can remember to preserve about the early days.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: This here has been a great help to me. It's a good thing it was roped there. See I have no work certificate. See when I was born it wasn't super -typical about that then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was talking to someone down there in Morrison and they said have you got a bible?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I said yes. If there's anything roped in that then I said it's all roped. That's how I got the dates on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Beyond the dates and all that, what can you remember about old parents and what it was like for them starting in here and why they decided to come out from Moscow?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I can kind of give you a hint. My dad and somebody else come out from Moscow in the fall and built a cabin and ruined the family in the next year. Another friend Willie was born you see.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I tell you, these old pioneers, same as any other country that had pioneered, they had a tough day of it. It wasn't smooth you know. Of course, they'd get a cabin built and you had to clear so much land within a certain length of time. I can't just explain that. I think it was two acres you had to have. I know there was a certain amount of ground you had to have. I guess I can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember? Do you know what made your father decide to leave Moscow and come out here in Homestead?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He was raised in the timber back in Norway. And when he seen the true EYA, here's where I stayed. And the country around Moscow at that time was taken. But the next first prairie over there at Craigmont was thrown open. And dad was here in time that he could almost sit on the next first prairie. Which he was sorry ever after he hadn't done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why? What was better about that than here?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You see that, you would start right in power there in October.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And out here with Troy, I heard dad tell that. They hired some saddle horses in Moscow and two or three more and they were riding through looking at the country you know. And they camped at Spring Valley. On the seventh day of July there was half an inch of ice on the water. And they said no, no, no, we don't want this place.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: So they kept looking and they found this place.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah. Yeah. And one reason that made him take part of this place is because there's two good springs here in the canyon. And he seen that water and the rest is all rocks on there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there people out here when he came out already?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: A couple of experts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were the only people around Park? Huh? They were the only people around Park at that time? So he was really one of the first sellers?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He was among the first. There were two bachelors in there ahead of him and Ferguson. And there was some at the name of Ferguson. I can just barely remember them. And most of these homesteaders in here, well I said most, but a good fifty percent of them were bachelors. I better be careful about that. I got in bad over that one time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were these two guys like? Do you remember anything about them? Do you remember anything about these guys that were here first?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, I don't really. Because when I got big enough to pay any attention or to listen, I had to hold up the skidoo when it come. I tell you, I know this much and you do too. And it was all a gathered difference from now. Now it's the almighty cash and expenses where you go. I can remember as a kid, if you were sitting up there and had to have a little lift, you know, maybe for a day or two, doing something that had to be done. The whole neighborhood would be there. And nobody paid any attention. Maybe next time they come and help me. And it was through that help one another that they got by at all. And now you go to one of these neighbors who wanted to help you and they want to go for five hours an hour.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And I don't blame them personally because there's a system we got into.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: So they...

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: it was really just by helping each other that people would get by.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, that's the only way they got by, you know. Because they never had no money. If they didn't go out around Moscow in the harvest, there were jobs in the mines up north there. If they didn't make a few dollars, they had to go for a few rags to wear and a grub.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father used to have to work out in the early days when you were first out here?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he go?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He worked in the mines mostly, up north.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He wasn't at home a lot of the time, I guess.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, he couldn't be, you know. When he was home, he had to clear land or do something. And you know, them people enjoyed themselves. I can remember, you know, maybe on some Saturday night the women would make a bunch of sandwiches and some cake and coffee. We'd go to your house on the hill with you. I bet, sure. And play cards and dance or whatever you want to do. Have a visit in the social, you know. You can't do that now. We won't see it anymore. It was a week down here after I got big enough to go. Oh, I've come up here to dance with myself right up here in this, right up here, you know. In this schoolhouse. Sure, you bet. After me and I was married, I've been over here to the show then. I used to go when I was just a kid, 12, 13 years old. I used to be in the Quadrille Quarter. Yeah. Come on square dances. You probably remember that. No, I don't. No, I wasn't up here around here then, Charlie.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You were still around Harvard. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you have to do to call the quadrilles? Was that hard?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, it wasn't hard. You had to have a pretty good voice. It wasn't anything hard. You know, there'd probably be a fiddle and at least an organ and maybe two or three guitars to go on. You had to have a pretty strong voice, you know, for it to carry in one of the big halls. I wore a hair of Cedar Creek. I said big hall. It was big for them base. You could dance three sets at a time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you could, huh?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And really, you know, you had to have a pretty good voice. Yeah, you bet. And you had to be capable of talking fast. Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But, you know, I don't even know what a quadrille is. No. I mean, it was before my time. Yeah, you bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of dance is it?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, it's...

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, there's four sets. And each set, the boy and the girl is a set. And you have four of them in this.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Haven't you watched them on television?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, I don't know that I have. No, they're lined up in a square. Oh, I think, yeah. And when they play, then you throw out the command to join your hands and circle to the left and break and swing your opposite partner and all that kind of stuff. You look under the chief commander. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what kids would do for dating, go to dances?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I mean, is that... I'm trying to wonder what it was like for young couples or how the social life was. Well, they had waltzes and two steps along with their square dances, too, you know, at that same time. See, it wasn't all the square dances. They had two steps and waltzes. Yeah. And everybody in the community would get together at a dance.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You know, I don't know. I believe people were happier than they are now. Too much I don't have a dollar now.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Of course, I know it's a hand in me, but it's a change.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But I've heard other people say the same thing.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: That in some ways there is a lot more fun there. People did more together than they did. And you know, I can remember you knew them. You know, the old folks' wrists, you know. Oh, yeah. Well, they didn't homestead in here. They bought in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, they bought, huh?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But they got old and had a little sickness and one thing or another. Just to give you an example of what might happen to you, Charlie Andrew come down here. He said, you still got that big team. Yeah, we got them. He said, you know, them old folks' wrists haven't got any wood. Oh, they're wintertime, too. Well, he said, there's dry trees right below them. He said, I want to say tomorrow we'll go get them wood. Yeah. Well, tomorrow when we got there, I bet you there were six or eight men there. And we all cut wood that day. They had more wood than anywhere else in the country. And you never thought nothing about it. No. You know, if it hadn't been nowadays, anyway, it's hardly $25 for going down with that team.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, it hasn't been too many years ago that you remember when Pete Bumgarner, of course, he got killed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember when he got hurt?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah. And by golly, they said they was out of wood, so I had my four -wheel drive outfit, and Eddie McKinney had his four -wheel drive outfit. Oh, I don't know, there was Clay and Charlie Stratton and Eddie and Teddy and myself. There would be a bunch of the older men. Oh, yeah, you bet, man. Now, I'll tell you, when we got the first two scot loads of wood in there, by golly, Mickey, she just had two sticks of wood. That's all she had in the yard. And that was for, I don't know whether she had electric range or not. I know they had a wood heating stove. And by golly, she just had two sticks of wood. It was just running that close. Can you tell me any more about how your parents made do when they were first out here, so far as getting through the winter and just...

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, they all worked together, I guess. They finally got hold of a cow and finally got hold of a pig. Finally a few chickens and a little piece of ground cleaned up the duration of the feed on, you know. And of course, most of that fell on the older kids and the mother. Dad would be out working. Well, not only dad, but the men of the house would generally be working. But of course, that's the same story in any new country that was opened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did it get so he could just stay here and work and didn't have to go up to the mines?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, it took quite a while. In fact, too long. Of course, you know, Moscow was the closest trading place. Or Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't consider Troy or Deary?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, it wasn't any.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: There was no Hellmair, no Trolley. Or Deary. Deary, yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I won't believe it. Deary originated, I think, along about 1906. Yeah. And I don't know when Troy originated, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Helmer and Bogle, I think, was a little older town, wasn't it, Charlie?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, it couldn't have been much older. No, it couldn't have been much. No, six, see, that's what made these towns when the WINM comes through. See, that's what made them towns. There was no Troy and no Kendra.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, yeah. Right to Moscow. Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: See, there was Moscow and what we called Blue City.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You know, and then with transportation they had them days with horses, you know. You could take them to Northfield, though, and camp on the road. When, in the fall, they always made it a point in the fall to lay in a good supply of staple groceries. And they never bought flour in one of them little sacks. No, they bought about 20 of them. Hundred pound sacks. No.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Or 50 pound sacks, anyway.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And rice and things like that, you know.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, where did you work for, who did you work for first in the woods? Who did you work for?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Henry Fasher. Oh.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Did you know Henry?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I worked for him in 1922.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: What camp was he running when he was 14 years old? What camp was he running?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I worked right in camp 6, United Helmer. Oh, that's where he was at.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He was running that camp, huh?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: 16 years old. I'll tell you how I got in there. I went in there and I went up and asked him, you know, for a job. He looked at me a little bit and he said yes. He said, you're going to go in the ball game. The ball game is the ones that build the roads and do all that kind of work.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I was in the ball game 3 or 4 days and he come one day and he said, can you drive team?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I said, I don't know. I said, I've been around horses some but I couldn't skip logs. I said, of course, I've never done that. I said, I've skipped a few wood logs. Oh, he said, we've got a pretty gentle team here and I'd like to have you bunch for the drays, he called it. That was to get the logs all up in one bunch where they could load them on a different rig, you know. Like he said, I believe you could make out alright. I said, okay. Great big, but I had a heck of a time getting harnesses on them. Big, you know, big horses. And I went out and boy, I was lucky I got into a good crew, you know, the rest of them. They helped me a little, oh, quite a bit, you know, and if I done something wrong, they didn't howl about it. They just helped me explain about it. But I was used to these old devils and they didn't do anything just like the cowboys, you see, that's what I was scared of. But they got along fine. For the only one time we've been putting our head into a row. But I call that team Bird and Mabel. Fact, you know. And one of them, you know, he was kind of pokey, you see, he wasn't holding an upper end. I remember one thing that I was handy with was the end of the line. I took the end of that line and I brought it up to myself again. Didn't intend to do it that way, but I done it so quick, see. Not thinking. Take them, flash it. But she had a work under about that long, swelled up about that much where that strap come down.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I said, what happened there?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I said, she mopped it around, I said she wouldn't listen, she wouldn't mind and poke easy. I said, I just woke her up a little bit. I figured now it's when I get a headache. She said, you know, it's alright to wake him up, I said, I don't have used him that way.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: How long have I ever come home?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, he must be dead. Yeah, he must be.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, because, you know, in 1922 when I went to work down there at Camp 1 at Hatterford, he wasn't too young a man then. Yeah, no, in 1922 when we was farming on this sand all around here. Yeah, that's what I say. Yeah, that was in spring of 1922, then he went right from there to Princeton, Camp 1.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a guy was he?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I never heard about him. He wasn't too bad a man to work for. You're good to work for him. You bet, he was good to work for. You bet you.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any things about him that you can remember, what he was like, stories about him?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, I can't really. If you've done your work, you might come along and say good morning and keep on the go.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I've only seen that man once and I've worked for him quite a bit. When he really blowed up and he had reasons to blow up. A saw gang.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I guess I can say it here, there's no women around but they're sort of told to stick the crustets off his ass. Boy, he heard it right now. He said maybe you'd like to try shoving it up there. He never even can be. He did that.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I heard that.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I'd like to see it in you. Yeah, so would I.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: There's one man I never will forget.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I went to him and got in an argument over that strip. He said to me, you're going to make a man the majesty of any man I've ever seen. I said the only reason I'm not as mad as you are is that you're a darn much bigger. That's more obvious to be mad. You know, we jumped up and walked off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that argument over?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: The strip. It used to log, you know, we used to blaze out a certain amount of timber or a certain locality and we skidded it. We used to skidded it. We logged it, see. I call that strips. There you go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the fight about?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: The price on skidding. You see, you may have a good price for skidding, I may have a bad one and he was paying the same for all of them. See, that can be quite a variation there in the chance you've got. Like anything else.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I took a little advantage of Axel. I guess I should have done it, but he'd make me mad. Axel would have to have a damn good reason to have can to be. That is one thing that old Nauta thought I was okay with them horses. You bet. You're dang right.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: See, you worked the team for quite a while. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: How many years did you drive team?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, I was the go team there.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I worked for Axel Anderson for nearly 20 years. Yeah, that's what I'm saying. You started in when you were 16.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, you're still driving team up there, Charlie?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Still driving team there.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, when did you quit the woods? I mean, when did you come home to stay?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I quit the woods there in the 30s.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: In the 30s, huh?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, did you... Tell me about driving the teams, what that was like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have some teams that were a lot better than others?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I would imagine that you would. Yeah. And it depended on the know -how of the teamster. You know, some people, well, they had no mercy. They'd go out and murder a team in a month. I drove the same team all summer. They'd lose a little weight, I'll say that, but I lost a little weight too. But that didn't hurt them any to lose a little weight because they had all the winter to give it back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would make a team a good team?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Like I've heard that a good teamster can take a team that doesn't seem to be very good, the horses don't seem very well matched, and can really make them perform. It takes time and patience is all. In the first place, you've got to like horses. You bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: In the second place, you want to study the disposition of your horse. It's just like running a crew of men. You bet. They're good men, but you've got some in that crew that you've got to handle just a little bit easier than this fellow over here. You can talk a little rough to him and he'll just like that too. Don't do it to him. You know, there's a difference in disposition in horses like there is in people. There's some people you've got to lead and some you've got to drive, you know.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And the same thing, you've got to go with them horses. I've seen good horses, I absolutely refuse to fall. I knew it was pretty good a couple of weeks ahead of that.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, you must have drove a team pretty dang close to 40 years, Team

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: They just buggered them up and fooled them, that's all. And they may be of a little nervous disposition and probably, you know, they'd give them a licking or something and jerk them and yank them around. You can make a horse mean them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me about how you handled your teams?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, that's pretty hard to explain. I never overloaded them and I never pulled them outwind. And lots of times I had hard pulls for only moving maybe that much at a time for just a little ways, you see. But I took my time of doing it, I didn't get excited and hurry. And you can get them horses so they've got just as much confidence in you as you would have in a man.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: It's really a gift.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I say, what did you do on the park sleigh -haul?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I saw it.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, you saw it?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I actually only saw it here on the road section. And just about the time it broke up there, I was going to start driving Tow Team. And Henry said to me, he says, come up. Yes, tomorrow I said to start driving Tow Team. And they said, right here, I'd kill them but I can't. And I got the next day they shut down. Well, the road was gone, you know.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: See, I worked on the section that day. See, I never went to the woods till May in 1922. That's when I come to the woods is in May in 1922. And that's just when Nago took over. T .P. Jones and just left and Nago took over, you see. And see, I was on the section. I was on the section for two years.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Where you were?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: On the Ale Hill, see. I worked on the Ale Hill for two years. Yeah, I got four bits an hour working down there on the section. Yeah, and these fellas here, they tell me the two horse skinners got 32 cents an hour and the four horses got 37. That's it. Yeah, and I was working on the section down there. I was getting four bits an hour like a day. Four and a half for four horse skinners. Yeah, and I went to his department in 1922. That was the biggest mistake ever made in my life. Oh.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yep.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Back in the World's War, you remember when Lewis had that salt medal up north of Mobile?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, you bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: What was that medal?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Feather Creek.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Huh? Well, you mean up there?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, he was on Feather Creek and then he moved up there. He was on Feather Creek. Yeah, and then he moved up on the East Fork. Mike Anderson, you know, his droppers. Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He come here with that old dog, you know, and that dog for just a jumpin'. So he said, throw a few rags in the back sack. He said, we're gonna have you on the turndown in the summit. And I said, yeah. Oh, I went up there and we saw them because they changed sauce, you know. Changing sauce.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I said, what do you pay on that turndown?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Thirty -seven and a half cents an hour. Oh, I said, go to hell. They turned it down, oh, 37 and a half cents an hour. You know, I finally talked him up to 45.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, did you?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No. You see, when he had the sawmill in here, I turned it for three different summers. You know, there's a knack for that.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I'll bet there is. You bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah. I wish I'd never learned the knack. I thought it was a matter of my back now. Yeah, I'll bet it is. You bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: How did it get her? What does it to her?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: They're turning them logs. You see, when the log goes on a carriage, it goes through the saw, they take off a slab. The next time they'll probably take off a board. Then they'll flip it so that face is down and then start outside again. It's my job to turn them. Instead of having mechanical turners like everything is now, I... It was round -headed turners.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And most generally, you know, they didn't want you to monkey, either, do they?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, you bet not. Well, gosh, we average from the $39 ,000 a day there in that match stock. Whew! Of course, it was a big Denver.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, yeah, you bet big white wine.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Beautiful Denver. Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And before I forget about the horse, being a youngster, I wondered about if you had anything to do with choosing the horses that would be your horses.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Were you going to Portland?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No. No, I worked for the county museum in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you decide what was a good horse? What qualities would you look for in a horse to be your team?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, no, that's pretty hard to explain that. You're putting it all the time to look at a horse and try to figure out whether he's high -liked or whether he's tricky or what, you know, and stubborn. There are certain marks on horses that, like people, you know, that give them away eventually.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like what?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, you take one that's got little pin ears, you know, and they stand straight up. You want to look out for him because he's stubborn. And just little things like that, you know. And you can tell, you know, if they're touchy, get away from them or they couldn't hurt you and make an unusual noise or something and watch them. You can kind of figure out their disposition.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I'll tell you. It's up here at Camp 5. The camp is on the highway going to Bovill.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And the rig that I was on was laid off. So Pierce, he was the walking boss, he says, you go up there to Camp 5 and Ratcliffe will give you a job. And he says, you'll be right handy if I want you, see. See, I was, and so I went up there and so I pulled the road for a while. And so the teamster quit. And I know the reason why he quit.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: So by golly, Clyde, did you ever know that team? They called them Jim and Star?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Mm -hmm. Well, that's the team that I took with Jim and Star.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so I was the fellow by the name of Pete Olson. He was the swamper. And like Charlie said, I was, see, the future was grand and I was bunching for him. And in the future, see, the skidway was getting pretty well filled up. He'd call the swamper and want him to go down the full road. Well, that'd leave me up there alone.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so...

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: They were wide wheels. They were made out of wood and then they got three iron bands around them, see. And so the skid was up about this high, see, so you were all bummers. But then we was using drays and the drays, they only got that high off the ground. Well, you know, I'd always pick my logs and help them thread the best I could, you know. And so by golly, you know, and the skid, it was pretty steep too. And I always, I picked him pretty good bunk load, you know, so he could get a good top load. And by golly, you know, here I come down and he never even left me as much as a bumper log.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You know, he never left me as much as a log out here, you see, a bumper log, you see, so I'd have something to roll again. Well, I'll tell you what he did. He took and rolled the whole darn works on, putting all of them on. And then he put his wrapper, what we call a chain, that went around her and he got up on top and then he spread the wrapper. Then he put three logs on top of the wrapper, see, and there he was. And old Frank and Tint, they couldn't even get off the landing with it. So by golly, he hollered and I come down and so I just took my line and had a swamp hook in the end, see. So I just took and went back on the back end of the drays, see, and put my swamp hook into that, went up alongside of the drays, see, with my team and got him out of there and away he went. So then I went up in the woods and I took and lined me a great big white pine butt out, the biggest one I could find, so I got out there in the woods and so I took blocks and I eased the darn thing. I got right out and pickled into that skidway and I blocked her. I went back up in the woods and set out. And so then I put two or three logs more behind it and then I went up in the woods and set out. And you know, Fred, he tripped that big log in when it went down and there he went to bunk on his drays. So he had to go clear the back of the camp and get him a new dray and by the time he got back I had lots of logs on the skidway. But say he always left me a bumper log after that, I learned him something. You bet, I learned him something.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Do you remember, I don't remember the year, up at Elkhart, a big camp A. Just above camp I had a big student suit there, only like twelve thousand of a million. And I remember you were hooking on the Marion. Yeah, that was in 26. In 26, and something went wrong with the Marion and you come over to me.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And I had a picture of you and me and the team where you had that skip like that, the skip door. I was up there either two years, I could have been up there two, either in 26 or 28.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I come down off my hill sometimes for 28 logs. You bet, you bet. Got me a record of it. Yeah, you bet. Just like that. Just like that. You know. Go on, go on. Only twelve thousand, a million and a half, during the summer. And somebody said, only like twelve thousand you still haven't that much. And somebody said in there in the crowd, if I was that close I'd be sure hustled around and found them twelve thousand. I said it wouldn't have made any difference, I said the skater would have stolen it anyway. And by the end of all night I was standing right there and I didn't see him. He said, you ought to know you'd have an answer. You bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But you know that's over in North Timber. You bet, you bet, you're right. It was really worth writing it part time on the back end. Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: We went out to work one morning when it was 40 and I'm below zero, but we didn't stay out very long.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yep, I'll never forget that. I was down there, I had two cats down there for Charlie Clark, performing during 40 and I had a 40 caterpillar dozer. And so Wayne Wilson was one cat driver and Charlie Clark's son was the other, and so here comes Charlie through the bunkhouse. He says, you're going to have to take that 40 out this morning. I says, what's the matter Melvin, he's sick. Well, I said, it's an awful good morning to be sick. I went out and looked at the thermometer hanging alongside the cookhouse and it was just clicking 49 below zero. And so I said to Charlie, I said, then it used to be me taking that dozer out, I said, I can't start it. He says, you don't need to worry, he says it's been running all night. It was too. But we went out and got one dragon. We come in and shoot horses that was coming in too. That's when we shut down for a while, to have warmed up.

words: Because that team that Charlie Hickman drove, I guess, I don't know where they come from, but they hadn't even gotten fattened up yet from that park's lay -off and Charlie had them down there at Camp One. speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that there was much difference in the different kinds of horses that you used? Different breeds?

words: Well, I did, yes. Now, the different breeds of horses would have had different elements. Now you're taking like the Perchons. The Perchons were good, nice, as General Ruhle and General Horst said, but their front feet would never last under them. Their front feet would go haywire on them. Wouldn't stand up. You take for a horse that was really tough, it shouldn't be a full rut of no kind. If you could cross breed a coyote, you'd get a big horse, you'd have the best horse. You bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know what was the matter with their build that made the front feet go bad?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, we couldn't figure out just the nature of that breed of horse, but there was some way that they were set up. Well, there wasn't a more bit of a horse than the Perchon though. You take the Belgians and the Shires, they were good horses. But until they lent them Shires, they were bullheaded. I know it. You bet. Just like a bullheaded person.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But when you got them then, you had a good team then.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You bet. I'll tell you what I meant with Jerry Smith. He said, you know, some of them pot -miced horses' legs were that big around, she raised it off about 18 inches. Oh, I said, no, no, no, no, no, no. I tell you, if they had lots of long hair on them, they'd stay straight out. Yeah, that was that Shire in the kleistwil, you know. Yeah. She'd see them around the wine up there. See, her and her daughter are in Japan. Yeah, that's what they tell me, yeah. Jerry's back then.

words: Would you have much trouble with the different kinds of horses working together, or could you run like four different kinds of horses on your 4 -horse team?

words: Oh, yeah, you could get different horses with different breeds with different breeds. words: Well, there's a picture of them.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But in poetry, they're one of us. And we have to get around to them and in the crowd and they might just go some haywire. So if you're going to a poem contest you want to put that team in with all the horses you see in the country to get them used to other horses.

words: They sent me and Charlie Wiley out with the horses and three or four men to the swamp forest to build some landings. And we went to work there packing out, brushing, cleaning up, rubbish and fixing up, you know. And all but once that horse went straight in the air and we heard tin rattles, you know, hitting taunts like that onto the bottom of that bucket and his foot went through the bottom and he had the bucket up there on his leg. But now you talk about a performance. You know that horse went some crazy.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: It was funny after it was over but it wasn't funny while it was going on.

words: You got him quieted down and got that bucket off of him. I bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you take care of the horses when you were logging with them? When would you feed in water?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: They were fed in water three times a day. They were in barn at night. A man picked out to stay in the barn with them. He fed them and looked after them in night shift. On the day shift we packed and flaked the hay in a little drain with us out in the woods and fed them for dinner. Of course, us men, the biggest percentage of us ate other lunch buckets. They were all packed lunches. And that's the same way the horses, they got that, the mokes and hay. I'll tell you there's one thing about the pot I'll give them credit for that. They took care of their horses.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You didn't see no hay racks around there?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No. They fed good. The nago was pretty darn good for everybody's horses. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard from one of the nago's boys that he was a jockey before he came out here.

words: He had actually been a jockey himself.

words: He was too big a man for a jockey.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Gosh.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Why, he was a man who weighed 180 pounds. Yeah, that's the one I was going to say. He weighed 180 pounds. I was going to say the nets had big horses. Well, of course, like I say, I didn't know him until 1922, but shoot. I knew him for just exactly 20 years. That's how long he was superintendent, was 20 years. From 1922 to 1942. Yeah, he was 20 years. He was probably longer than T .P. Jones. I guess he was probably longer than the old superintendent. I mean he stayed on the superintendent lower than any other man they ever had.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I got tickled one time in Camp D in Elk River. They couldn't get teams to find a way to take a team if they didn't give up until they did, you know. Although he was swamping for me, I got another swamper and they took the team. He was bunched into a chute. And that one time when I was with the bucky, he wouldn't do nothing, you know. And you know, Willie was a little hot -tempered. And Willie, he got hold of a club there, a really good one. And boy, he took that horse right behind the ear with that club. He threw his head over the other horse, you know, and knocked him flat. And when the horse got up, there stood an awful. And the awful thing said, I wouldn't do that.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well Willie said, what would you do with a son of a bitch like that?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He said, kill him. So he wasn't unreasonable. But that horse didn't last in camp long. They took him out and sold him for a deal. Make bait for something, I don't know what they done with him.

words: No, they didn't work them any harder and they got everything that they needed to eat.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And then there was a veterinary come in, oh, they come in about once a week, didn't they, Charlie?

words: Once a week, once every two weeks at least. And if they had a sick horse, he'd be right in there right now. Yeah. If they had a sick horse, he'd be in there right now. You see, the company hired their own veterinary. Hired their own vet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would a horse be able to work? How many seasons could a horse take?

words: Well, that depended on them. That could be anywhere from one to five, six. You bet. Would they get better and better the longer they worked as far as... Well, no, I wouldn't exactly say they'd get any better unless they were terrible young when they started them. Yeah. No. Now if they'd start the three -year -old, he'd be a bigger and better horse than a four -year -old. Yeah. But now if you started the four -year -old, he wouldn't be no better at six. No.

words: Of course, if a horse looked darn good, he'd buy an eight and nine -year -old, though. He would, though. But they had to look pretty good then. He just couldn't have had to know them.

words: Well, there was a blacksmith that damn near ruined the team that Nago bought. By golly, but the blacksmiths got tanned. And I worked the horse, but he got a rope burn. words: But I mean, in this case, what did he do that was wrong?

words: Well, he roped this horse, see, and he let him rope burn, see. You bet. He roped him and let him rope, rope. Imagine he tied his leg back, see, and let him.

words: When you walked, you'd be a bit this way. I knew him, yeah. One shoulder was dropped on him. He was a big Russian. Surly sort of a guy. Big husky man. Yeah, you bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You bet. You bet. Oh, man.

words: You betcha. So that put me on crutches, and then the next time my own dang foolishness, when I got through in that deck of logs up there, Woeeater's deck of logs, that was all foolishness. That was all my fault.

words: You remember that, don't you?

words: You bet. Well, you see, we had used the track layer, and the darn steel would come back underneath the machine, see. Then he'd kick her head. Well, everything went fine and we got ready to go home that night and the darn thing stuck over about that far in the end of the deck, over the side of the car, and like a dang nut I didn't pick up the end of it and throw it off in the car. I and Gwyn Maynard were settin' on the darn thing, and that thing hit that deck of logs, see. Started the deck of logs rollin', and it went off on one side of the car and hit his head on a rock. Threw me into that deck of logs and that deck of logs was rollin', and there was a white pine about so big around that's the only thing that saved me from going back under the car. That thing rolled down on top of me and held me there. And there wasn't no bones broke, but there wasn't a place on the body, it wasn't black and blue. Oh, geez, gosh, the back of my head, it was all cut to pieces. Had it sewed up in seven different places, seven different places, and there wasn't a place that, oh, I was just bruised up all over. words: The first horse I ever had was a buckskin Caius. My brother -in -law gave it to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you talk about something whiter than down there, what's one?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But you know there was no cow around or out around her. Of course, I was only about 12, 13, 14 years old, I don't remember. I helped that Hickman and Kimberly, Hickman and Kimberly, the cattlemen down in Juliaetta. Further down there. That way. And they come up in here and they bought up cattle, you know. Maybe they bought three or four head off from this one on, three or four from that one and so on. And they bunched them in our field overnight. They were going to walk them down. This has been years ago, you know. And they asked me if I'd follow them part way on the road until they got them cattle road -wise. And I followed them to Clearaw on Cedar Creek. And there was a farmer's lane there. And he went in and asked the farmer if we could put the cattle in the lane. And the farmer said yes. We put the cattle in the lane and they had a store over there at that time. So we said let's go in and get something to eat. I said, oh, we didn't get to eat in there, there'd be crackers and sardines and soda pop. Well, that would beat nothing. So we went in there, you know, and finally somebody said, there's a couple of your cows going back. The cow key went and the saddle horse was standing there, you know, and jumped on that saddle horse and took after that cow. And boy, I thought the cow was gaining on me. Well, I run to the Buckskin and old Kimberly was there and he said, there's no use for me to go. He said, because this horse is out running mine. And I said, it might outrun this one. I said, it'll run when it doesn't. And it took me about a mile to do it, but I overtook that cow and headed her. It was bending her back and her tongue was sticking out about that far. Old Hickman, he said to me, he said, kid, I'll give you a hundred dollars spot cash for that cow use. Of course, I wouldn't have took a thousand.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I come home, I told dad about it and he said, send her. Send her, he said, and we'll pick up another one someplace. We sold her, you know, and probably got one for $60, just as good.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Why don't we, Jerry, he's got a couple of pretty good saddle organs.

words: And blacks? Well, did Dwayne Thompson ever tell you about the time that they, all them farmers went in together and drove them and took them hogs?

words: I don't know where they took them. I think they took them to Grangeville somewhere off the Salmon River. Charlie Hickman, he was in that bunch.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, you were?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And that, Dorndorf, he was mixed in with them one time.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: God, I got to think back. Wayne was seven years old and he was 33 at the time now and I don't know when it was. I was with him, he went down and bought that seat guard for that D4 cat and Hickman was down there. He was, that Hickman, he was selling machinery for debtors there in Juliet. And I listened to that, so. Well, I'll tell you what I think it was, I think it was the spring of 65. Oh, that was really recent. Yeah, but I mean, but that, but I mean Dwayne was 33 years old that time and he was seven years old when they made the hog drive, so now you figure that out. So it would be, that would be about 40, more or less, it would be around 40.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah. That was, I got laid off, I got laid off in January and that was in, that was in March of 65. And Dwayne was seven years old when they made that hog drive. Hmm.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Our brother Willie, he had a Hamiltonian, a full -blood Hamiltonian inside the horse, you know, when she was a mare. So Dad, he decided to take her and go get the cows one night. And the cow took off out of the van and he wasn't going to go and he took after her, you know. And the cow was running, you know, and the horse right after it. All at once the cow switched and went that way. Well, the horse switched and went that way too, but Dad, he went that way. Heh, heh, heh, heh, heh, heh, heh, heh. It didn't hurt him though. But of course, that take no less came to see him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you use to get the, get the team going right on steep slopes? Did you have those hold back deals to stop them from going too fast?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or did they just go real slow on a real steep?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No. On them chutes, when they got slick in the fall, the chute was made in the shape of a trough like that, see. You used to drive a railroad spike about two -thirds in and bend them so their heads would stick up like that. And them logs riding over that, that, and them heads sticking up would catch and tear in the wood, you know, and slow them up and hold them. And then they had another rig. I don't know whether I can explain it or not. But anyway, it was fastened in three dogs that you fastened it onto the log. And up here in the middle it had a hook come up like that, see. And then you had a piece of chain, and I preferred one about so long, with a vague ring. And then you hooked that vague ring over this peg that stuck up on this rig, you see. And as long as you pulled straight ahead, you'd hook, see. But as soon as you pulled sideways, you'd unhook. And sometimes, you know, we'd have places fixed. You'd come, you know, and maybe you'd be in a good, stiff trough when you hit that place. You'd swing the team in, and that would swing around there and cut you loose from the logs, you see. They'd go on and you and the team would stay back. Like all them jig rats. Yeah, I bet. Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Do you know where that school mom shack used to be over there, this side of the show's middle there? Well, you know that pinnacle up there?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, Curly and I, Curly and Daley and I, we logged that pinnacle.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: We did?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah. We used 27 dogs in a day. Huh. Yeah, we got two drags a day. We'd go up in the morning and then by golly, we'd come down at noon and straighten out the skidway.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, you know, where you used that many dogs, my car's alone going back up the hill. Well, we had them hung all over them. They had them hung on the hems and everything else. Yeah. Well, I'll... You know, when you get to climbing like that, you bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so, by golly, Welch sent us up there. Bill Asplen went up and looked at it. Puckett went up and looked at it. And so, by golly, I've been working on the marine there at Camp 10, you know, Welp. Pierce brought me down there to Camp 1, he says. And he says, when I need you, I'll know where I can get you. So I was dead out on the sidewalk the next morning. And so, here come Welch. He was a foreman. Well, he says, you can go with Curly this morning. He says he's already done been up and looked at it. I said, all right. So we went up there. And so we worked for about two weeks. Well, here come Pierce one night with his feeder. And I went back to Camp 10. Somebody laid off up there. And I hooked on for a week or two. And then he brought me back down to Camp 1 again. And so I went in. I kept my same bunk. And so the next morning, I was standing out there on the sidewalk.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so I says, well, where am I going this morning?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Right back where you was. He said, I sent two different swampers up there with Curly. But he said, by golly, he said Curly wasn't going to do nothing until I went back. And I didn't. So I finished the job. And I'll have to tell you, he had a black team. And so there was a fellow by the name of, oh, he just died here, not too long ago. Living near the school mountain.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: A couple of wood mouts. Curly and I always get them over there to shack for him. And so when we come to lay off for Christmas, by golly, he was over there with a quart. And so I started hanging the dogs on the hams and getting ready to go to work. Well, of course, he had a fire going. And by golly, we sat there and nipped on that quart. Kept nipping on that quart. And so while I says Curly, I think we better get up on the hill if we're going to do anything. And so, you know, it was really laughable. That old black team knew him.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Get up.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Get up. Get up. You know, that team wouldn't tighten the tub. No, no, of course not. The team wouldn't even tighten the tub. He said, get up. So just what I did, I just took and unhooked the outfit, put him on one of them and sent him to camp. And I had my card up there above camp 10. And I come home. And I say, yeah, that black team wouldn't even tighten the tub. Oh, my gosh. So after Christmas, we pretty stuck the job down.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: They had one up in Alkimer, Bocky. And when he'd Bocky, he'd throw himself. So he licked him and poured water in his ears and everything else. I said to the fellow driving him, I said, if I was driving that horse, I said I'd get a rope. And when he'd thrown himself, I'd hold time so he couldn't get up and just let him lay there. My God, he said, it would work for you, but it wouldn't work for me. He said, I ain't acquainted enough here. We better not do that. And so when he was driving the horse, I didn't want to have too much say -so about it, you know.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And finally one day Axel said to me, he said, what would you do with that horse?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Some days he works and some days he don't, he said. I said, well, he throws himself that way. I said, I'd just hog tie him so he couldn't get up and just walk off and leave him. I said I'd leave him all night if I had to. By Jesus, he said, you know, we never thought of that. But they'd never done it. They finally took him out to go. He just stopped over and I didn't have it.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I guess I don't know Wargstrom.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You knew him, didn't you?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh yeah, you bet. I suppose he's gone too. Oh yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, the last I saw of him, he was up at camp 35.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, I see. Yeah. Mm hmm. Yep.

words: Were you working in the woods when the IWWs were going?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, I wasn't in there. In Dustin, Oregon. Yep.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You got tangled up with them the second time. No, six. But they didn't get no work.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I never heard about it, about 26. I mean, that was in 36. Oh, 36. I never even heard about that one, except I heard there was something out in the Algarve. You know, when the CIO struck here at the Meadows, I was appointed delegate that time. Oh yeah, that was in 47. Oh.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh yeah, sure. I was appointed delegate going to Portland. Yeah. You know, we done pretty well down there. Only damn near we talked about was the women we'd shrewd and the whiskey we'd drink. Yes, it was all bad. I heard it all about, we were delegates. I never seen such a goddamn fake in my life.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But that was a bona fide strike, though. The CIO had a real working agreement with the company. That was a bona fide strike. Yeah. But you see, in 36, you see, the IWWs, they didn't have no working agreement or nothing on them. See, the men, they just left on their own.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: See.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What went on in that one in 36? Huh? What went on in that one in 36?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, the men just, the ones that packed the cards just quit or else tore them up one or the other. I know a bunch of them tore up their cards. Yeah. Stayed right there.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Ernie Paul was one of them. He was barn boss there at Camp 32. He tore up his card. Mm -hmm. Oh, Claude Nyer left. Silent Joe left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oscar, that fellow from Cluse, Oscar, what was his name?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You know who I mean. Cecil Miller left. They lost a lot of their good men.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, you bet. All their good teamsters, a lot of them good teamsters left. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why did they leave?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, they was packing cards, see, and they voted to strike and the rest of us didn't, see. So consequently we didn't strike, but they went in the way. See.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, you know, there's a lot of times when I think Labour's got the dirty end of it. I thought so myself. Yeah. But there's a lot of times when I don't blame the company either.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh, but that time, Charlie, we just got back to work. Yeah. That's what I said.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Now, the fellow was swamping for me. He was a strong union man, you know.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And I told him, I said, what in the goddamn hell? I said, have you got any money in the tertiary?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No. Yeah. Yeah, you bet. He said, they'll have to feed us. I said, they don't have to. They didn't. But he said, I mean the government. I said, I don't give a damn who you mean. I said, they don't have to feed us. Well, they didn't feed me anyway. No. And I said, well, he said, they can go on a soup line. I said, a man should be goddamn proud of himself for the dread of a woman and a bunch of kids down on a soup line. You bet. But, uh... And I told him, I said, this is the first time since the war that you could earn a nickel. You bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I said, I believe in unionism and I believe in a man sticking up for his rights. But I said, we should have a few dollars in our pocket when we do. Well, you know, inside of ten days, that whole crew had been broke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was this struck? Which one was this, the 36 one?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Thirty -nine. Thirty -nine. Huh.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I made ninety dollars there pretty easy. But I'm not proud of it. I just assumed it wouldn't have been that way, but I couldn't help it. Yeah. You see, they shut her down, you see. Sure, you bet. Yeah. The white guard finally got called back. And they called from home, somebody. And they said, bring the swamp for her with you. So I brought Willie with me, see. Well, you know, the other swamp that I had that walked out had ninety dollars coming. Well, they got down there and gave that to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They did, huh?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Sure, they were from home, you see.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Huh.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But there in 36, though, back there, they'd just come in and... Well, the guy that was up there, he was there at 32. He couldn't even talk English. And he politely told us, we didn't leave, they was going to run us out, see. Well, that just wasn't done. But there was a bunch of kids, brush pilots and stuff in there that scared them out. And they did go up the stripes, see. But then they went down to 31 and they never got nowhere at all with that.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Born in the, uh, in the Mount Granerson, he was running a camp and I had them two cats up there.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so, he says, uh, what'll I do with that outfit?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He says, uh, well, we're going to shut down the Fourth of July anyway. So he says, well, just go up and pick up your riggin' and come down and... And he says, I'll let you know when we come back. And he's only off for four or five days and they called us when we went right back to 32 and went to work with it. Nothing had happened. But you see, down at 31, they never even voted to strike down there. Uh huh.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, was it still the same, uh, some of the same guys that had been IWWs in the 20 strike that were still the same guys in the 30s?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, I don't think so. No, these are, no, no. Oh, they could have been a few. I don't know. Oh, there was a few. They probably could have been a few. There could have been a few.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, what was the feeling about that? I mean, did you, did you have a lot of respect for, uh, for the IWWs in the, in the 30s?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Or, or was there, was it just the guys that were members and just... Well, well, you see, what had happened if they had a, if they had went out, it would have been a wildcat, a wildcat strike. Because, you see, the IWWs didn't have no, they didn't have no working agreement, none, none whatever with it. No charter at all. No charter or nothing, see, so what they, what they wanted to do, they were just going to come in and shut the company down, see. Well, they did, they did a lot of the camps down on the headquarters side. But, you see, we wanted, we was running two camps on this side, Camp 32, Camp 32 and Camp 31. You was down at Camp 31. Now it's Camp 32.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And, shoot, we just got back to work from... Well, over here on the Clearwater Inn, we had got desperate. Yeah, I did, you bet. A fellow swamped for me a few days in here at 31. And, uh, he went to take a crack. Up there in the brush. And I noticed, you know, when he took his pants off that he had a wide bandage across the small of his back. But, he swamped and worked alright and never complained or nothing, you know. I thought there was something wrong with him, but, you know, you kind of hate to ask a fellow too, you know. But, anyway, I was eating our lunches and laughing and talking.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He said, uh, I said, what happened to your back?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Forgot myself and asked it, you know.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: What happened to your back?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He said a bunch of the boys over on the Clearwater used a butt chain on me. A butt chain is a piece of chain about that long that's in the end of the tug. He said they used a butt chain on me. God damn it, he was scarred up like he was running through a chuck man. Sure, you bet. And what they beat him for, I didn't want to ask any more questions, you know. It's kind of a shame I didn't ask that one. So I didn't ask him anything more about it. But he was a good swamper, good worker, nice fob. He didn't tell me a little bit, though. He said he had a woman and three kids to support.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I had a woman and one, I'll tell you that. But although they did, uh, they brought a deputy sheriff in and this half -Moody, he's retired now, he got to be sheriff, but he's deputy sheriff. And they brought him in up above camp 32 and O .K. Nelson and others. There was three of them that came up there and stayed all summer.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: We never was bothered, we drove back and forth in there, we never was bothered. We could see a few IWWs once in a while, there was a few of them sticking around, but we never was bothered.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Dick Binge and Herschel Tribble both told me that in, uh, the strike after World War I, the IWWs didn't mind if a guy wanted to work. If a guy said he had his family to support, they respected him. That's what they said. Yeah, but this is a different bunch this time that come in over there. They were just really...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was pretty different, huh?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You better go and get it there, but they were just going to just shut the company down, that's what they wanted to do, you see.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: What used to shut me are some of them, unless it was striking. Everything the company done was dirty and wrong, but anything they done was all right. You bet. There was one there at, uh, Lewis's Mill, you know, and you know, we packed lunches. And then coffee and sandwiches set out there and you packed them in a bucket, you know. Boiling coffee in a bucket. Of course, filling one of them thermos bottles is pretty easy to get them a little too full. Pour out a little bit on the floor. And, uh, I said to the cook that Lillian Johnson was cooking, boy, there wasn't better grub in the state of Idaho than we had there.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I said, why don't you set a pan out there, and have them pour that coffee in the pan instead of on the floor? Why God, has anyone ever thought about that?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: So they set out a pan there, you know, if you had too much coffee and you bothered to dump out, you'd be in the pan. They'd stand there and they'd dump it alongside the pan on the floor. And I got it. Finally they had one of them meetings and they brought that up and, by God, I come back about what they were doing in the cook house, too. I said, instead of appreciating that cook house, I said, appreciating the cook that's in there cooking. I said, I tried to work with them. I said, do anything to make it a little pleasantry for them. And I said, you fellas, I said, you must have been racing a hog pen or something.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I said, what in the hell is the matter with you?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Some of them said they was going to lick me. Well, I said, that's alright, too, but I said, you're still dumping your stuff on the floor when it's on the call for it. Well, you know that's on the call for it. Another thing that helped the flunkies out, but it had a lot to do, your fork and your knife and your spoon leave them right here, all three in a pipe. I've seen them, they'd leave one there and the other one over here and the other one over here. Well, it takes twice the time for the funky to gather it up. And the fellow that ate there, it'd only take him a few seconds too late in a pipe. And, you know, things like that count. But grown men could be so dumb. Well, it wasn't dumb, it was meanness. Pour coffee along side of that pan, they knew what that pan was there for. And them same fellows were yelling about the potlatch.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You know, it don't take much, now you take like flunking, where they could grab all of them at once and everything was sent out handy. You know, it makes quite a difference when you're busy.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And I suppose they only had one cook and one flunker too, I suppose.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Two flunkies?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: They had two flunkies, I suppose.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And they had to wash dishes too?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, they had to wash dishes, no, they didn't have a dishwasher there.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And Lillian Cooks?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You knew her, didn't you?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Oh yes, you bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: The Nordic queen?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah. But boy, she could cook. She could cook, I bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, she was flunking up there at Camp 4 in 1923 up there for Frank James.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And then the next one that come in was Sturm's wife, Goldie.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I used to laugh at her by lying. She lives in Moscow, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the feeling about that strike in the 1920 strike or whenever that was right around then?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, you see, neither one of us was in the woods at that time. Of course, like you say, Herschel and Dick Benz, they were there.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But in 1936, we just bought one ready to quit. We kept running out of work. Of course, then in 1947, we had to go out. See, that was a bona fide strike. That was a bona fide strike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the issues in that one? That was the one when you went to Portland, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there issues in that strike that got settled?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I didn't quite understand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there issues in that strike that you guys settled? You were a CIO man, right?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yes.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You know, if I stay in one position to box the line, I get hurt right through here. If I drop that knee, I don't think it'll ever be alright. No, it won't be alright. It bothers me all the time. It broke it. It broke my knee right straight through there. I had two kneecaps. I split the kneecap.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I'll tell you, about the only thing that they accomplished in that 1947 strike, they got the top bunks cut off. That's about the only thing that they accomplished. They did get the top bunks cut off. Some double deckers that were single. Yeah, let's do that. You bet. That's about the only thing they did get accomplished. I'll tell you, though, they made pretty nice bunks. Oh, they made good bunk houses, yeah. Handy. Of course, they had pretty good bunk houses before that, but they still had the double bunks. Yeah, and too many men. Too many men, yeah. But that's about the only thing that they did get that settled in 1947. It was down there, it was 36.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Welsh.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: What was his first name?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Milford. Milford Welsh, for German camp, you bet. Well, he wasn't logging, he was building ropes. Yeah. And we saw a pushover there, I heard of it. Yeah. Boy, they had one bunk in this corner and one in that corner and one in this one, four men in a shack.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, a shack would be as big as this room, wouldn't it?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, you bet. It wouldn't be quite as wide, but I believe it was a little longer. It is longer.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: See, I made it all, they did accomplish that. That's one thing, that's about the only thing they did accomplish. And believe me, it was pretty nice. Yeah, they did accomplish that.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You didn't have to be flying to a piece in order to get up there.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Yeah, but as far as wages are concerned, I don't know. Might have got a week's vacation or something, I don't know, there wasn't too much. But that was, I would say that was a major issue in that, was getting them top bunks cut off. They did do that.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I want to remember when they stood for the pump lights. Yeah, you bet. You know, he was highball.

words: But you know what kind of life thing?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, I'll tell you, I can tell you one thing about Milford Welch. He never asked a man to do anything that he wouldn't do himself. No, no he wouldn't. He never asked a fellow to do anything that he wouldn't do himself.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And down there, you know, they were logging the timber out of the right -of -ways for them. Logs, you know. This Jim Lewis from Dover was driving a cat. Jim was just a kid, you know. I'll say Welch was over there talking to him and they had quite a talk. Finally Welch walked away and the kids started skidding. So we come over where we were sawing, we were sawing the roots off him, that pushover. And we talked a little bit. He said, boy, I'd like to see Jim Lewis learn a little more about the cats. You know, he said he ain't too bad, but he's just a little too nervous.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He said, why would Owen have a father to some talk with him?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: He said, don't look quiet down a little bit and look at it the second time. See, he took a knife into the kid and he was trying to help the kid all that he possibly could. Most of them would have said, these goddamn kids. That isn't what most of them would say. I heard one cat guard get told it after. The driver walked up to him. And he said, I heard you say that I'm nothing but a goddamn kid. And he said, I ain't nothing but a goddamn kid. And when it comes to driving a cat, he said, I suppose you knew just as much about it the day you was born as you do now. Of course, he said, I don't think you know a hell of a lot.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: The kid had guts, you know. Why, Owen thinks I could talk loud enough so we could hear him, you know. That kid's gonna get canned, but no sir, he stayed there till the camp. And after we drove cat a few days, maybe he got a little bitter. Now, he probably drove on road work, you know, or on a farm or something. He's having the cat all right, but you know, those little hits, lots of times, you know, if you hook onto a log here, it'll come out lots better than if you hook onto a log over here. There's where the kid was stuck, you bet. Excuse me for getting up, I gotta get up, I'll call it a night. Oh, sure. My eggs, pains.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: But I'll tell you another thing the world's done. We had an awful big rain down there in the world, I was there in the shop, and so he lost a culvert down there. So he radioed in and wanted 40 feet of two foot culverts. So Johnny come and got me and I, I looked them up. I took them down there and I found where the washout culvert was, turned my truck around. And there's about that much frozen dirt above it. And so, and then pretty quick here come Wilson, Bud, and Ogle. And Wilson's the only, that's the only man he kept. He turned all the rest of the crew loose. He kept his dozer operator in himself. And so, by golly, he, he broke that down. And you know that darn Welsh turkey choker, he jumped in the water just like that. And hooked them jumps out of there and, and I unhooked him up on the bank. And he was the foreman.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You bet.
