words: Well, just as quick as I see what was gonna happen, I went and started the truck, I started to motor my truck so that, so it'd be warm, you see, for us to eat lunch, you know. Of course, the only time I got wet too, but because it, how I come to get wet, I had to stand on that one end of the culvert and hold it down while Bud pushed some stuff in on top of it, see, before we got, I got wet. But of course, I was standing up there, I got, oh, I got wet up around there, of course, all right. Well, she was wet right to there. He jumped right into that darn cold water.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: You bet.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: So I said to him, I says, well, my gosh, Milford, I says, why don't you just hold a man here?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, he said, you know, he said, I got a small crew and he said, they're all riding together. So he said, I just turned them all loose.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I'm about to get jumped right up. Coming on home.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Went in there and, and then, well, I, and so Pierce sent me out there, Walt Pierce, Henry Jones and Perry Tout. Three of us went out together. And so I took a great team and Perry was swamping and Henry was bunching for me. And so by golly, one Saturday, he knew, here come, I had three sets of rollways, you know, it was filled up. So Sturgs will come out there just before dinner and he says, well, he says, you just might as well go home this afternoon. He says, I'll have a load break, come in there and load you out. And I says, all right. So we come back Monday morning and there wasn't no logs there. And so, oh, there's a place, oh, as far as from here off to that tree, the other side of that car, about that wide and about that part of the steel line. So Garsh and O, the mud was just like that. I knew better, but I took them, jumped my team right out to that mud and I went right out there and went right to the steel and loaded that little log. Sturgs would come and hunt cotton. And he says, you know, you can't do it. I says, I've done it once, yeah, but he says, you're not going to do it anymore.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so I says, well, what am I going to do?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: Well, and so he says, well, there's a landing up here. And so I went up and looked at it. And it was room enough for four rollways we'd have to put in rollways to see in corduroy the back end of it, see.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so I says, can we do it with the air or does it go with the job?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And he says it goes with the job. And about that time, here come Henry and the Swamper down. They wondered what happened to me. And so I said to Henry, I said, there's just one thing about it. I said, we're either going to have to do something different or go up here and put in four sets of rollways and corduroy the back end of it.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: So Henry spoke up and so he says, does it go with the job or do we get up to ten?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And the Sturgill says it goes with the job.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so Jones says, well, where do you want this rigging left?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And so Henry dropped his buttcheeks and so did I. So we went over the hill and went over there to Camp 14 and went to work for Dr. Grant. We had to walk miles all we did, went over one hill, dropped in to Camp 14. But in the meantime though, we had to wait until noon to get our checks, get scaled up what we had.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: So Sturgill, he took and laid my check face down and he says, hey Lancaster, he says, will you ever work for me again?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I says, I'll never work for you again as long as the man of the state of Idaho would work for us. So everything went along fine until 1931 when they laid us off and two loading rigs come into Camp 6 and we had to go pile and brush. So we went in there that morning up here to Camp 6 and we all signed up and went down to 2 -7 and got our axes. He let me get down the track at least a good 50 yards and then he hollered at me. He says, hey Lancaster, he says, come back here.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: I went back and he said, you know what you told me in 1924?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And I says, yes Sturgill. And I says, you're the last man in his left to work for you. Ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha. Ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha. Yep. He never forgot it. Five guys. So he, then he took and picked out about 20 men.

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: And he said, you see that draw there?

speaker: Charles Jelleberg or Carl Lancaster
words: No, he says, well you just go up there and start piling and brushing. And he says, there's five cents an hour more in it for you. Yeah, he did.

words: Well, he was a cat foreman for this other fellow. See, he'd been cat foreman for years and years and years. I don't know how many years. And, well, you see, and he was a cat foreman. And he was breaking on a Chez engine down there and, by golly, they'd just come along and took him right off the train, went and took him down there to Camp 39, made a camp foreman out of him. See, he'd never even been, see, that's it, you see. So I still say it was Milford's job. Uh -huh. You bet Milford's, well, it's his job. You bet it should have been his job.
